abstract: Feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) are major global pathogens that significantly compromise the health, longevity, and overall well-being of domestic cats. This study investigated the prevalence of these retroviruses among shelter cats in Portugal to better understand their epidemiological dynamics and clinical implications. Blood samples were collected from 326 cats during routine medical screenings upon shelter admission and analyzed using immunochromatographic assays. The overall seroprevalence for at least one of the viruses was 15.3% (50/326; 95% CI: 11.6–19.7). Specifically, 9.8% of the cats tested positive for FIV (32/326), 2.1% for FeLV (7/326), and 3.4% were co-infected with both pathogens (11/326). While infection rates for FIV and co-infections were notably higher in adult cats, no significant statistical associations were observed regarding sex, breed, hair length, or geographic municipality. As the first comprehensive report on retroviral prevalence in Portuguese shelter populations, these findings underscore the urgent need for enhanced surveillance, stricter adherence to vaccination protocols, and robust management strategies to control viral transmission within the feline population.
